<title>Grothendieck topologies sites</title>
<link rel="stylesheet" href="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/katex@0.16.11/dist/katex.min.css">

<script>
(function(){
  function renderAll(){
    if(typeof renderMathInElement!=='function'){ return setTimeout(renderAll,50); }
    renderMathInElement(document.body,{
      delimiters:[
        {left:'$$',right:'$$',display:true},
        {left:'$',right:'$',display:false},
        {left:'\\(',right:'\\)',display:false},
        {left:'\\[',right:'\\]',display:true}
      ],
      throwOnError:false
    });
  }
  if(document.readyState==='loading') document.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded',renderAll);
  else renderAll();
})();
</script>
<style>
  :root{
    --bg:#0f1218; --panel:#161b24; --panel2:#1c2230;
    --ink:#e8ecf1; --mute:#9aa4b2; --line:#2a3242;
    --yellow:#ffd866; --blue:#58c4dd; --green:#83c167;
    --pink:#e07a5f; --violet:#b39ddb; --cyan:#7de0d6;
  }
  *{box-sizing:border-box}
  html,body{margin:0;padding:0;background:var(--bg);color:var(--ink);
    font-family:-apple-system,BlinkMacSystemFont,"Segoe UI",Inter,Roboto,sans-serif;
    font-size:17px;line-height:1.65}
  a{color:var(--blue)}
  code{font-family:ui-monospace,SFMono-Regular,Menlo,Consolas,monospace;
    background:#0b0f16;padding:1px 6px;border-radius:4px;color:#f0c987;font-size:0.92em}
  main{max-width:880px;margin:0 auto;padding:4rem 1.5rem 8rem}
  h1{font-size:2.4rem;letter-spacing:-0.01em;margin:0 0 0.4rem}
  h2{font-size:1.6rem;margin:3.2rem 0 0.8rem;color:#fff;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);padding-bottom:.4rem}
  h3{font-size:1.15rem;margin:1.8rem 0 .6rem;color:var(--yellow)}
  p{margin:0.7rem 0}
  .sub{color:var(--mute);margin-top:0;margin-bottom:2rem}
  .widget{background:var(--panel);border:1px solid var(--line);border-radius:10px;
    padding:1rem 1.1rem 1.2rem;margin:1.2rem 0}
  .widget .hd{display:flex;justify-content:space-between;align-items:baseline;margin-bottom:.6rem;gap:.8rem;flex-wrap:wrap}
  .widget .hd .ttl{font-weight:600;color:#fff;font-size:.95rem;letter-spacing:.02em;text-transform:uppercase}
  .widget .hd .hint{color:var(--mute);font-size:.85rem}
  .row{display:flex;gap:.6rem;flex-wrap:wrap;align-items:center;margin:.4rem 0}
  .row label{color:var(--mute);font-size:.88rem}
  input[type="text"],input[type="number"],select{
    background:#0b0f16;color:var(--ink);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:6px;padding:.35rem .55rem;font-family:inherit;font-size:.95rem}
  input[type="range"]{accent-color:var(--yellow);flex:1;max-width:260px}
  button{background:var(--panel2);color:var(--ink);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:6px;padding:.35rem .7rem;cursor:pointer;font:inherit}
  button:hover{background:#242c3e}
  button.active{background:var(--yellow);color:#111;border-color:var(--yellow)}
  .readout{background:#0b0f16;border:1px solid var(--line);border-radius:6px;
    padding:.6rem .8rem;font-family:ui-monospace,monospace;font-size:.92rem;color:#cbd5e1;
    min-height:1.6em;white-space:pre-wrap;word-break:break-word}
  .pill{display:inline-block;background:var(--panel2);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:999px;padding:1px 8px;font-size:.82rem;color:var(--mute);margin-right:4px}
  .note{border-left:3px solid var(--violet);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#d8d2ef;background:#1a1828;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  .ok{border-left:3px solid var(--green);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#d5ebc8;background:#1a2418;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  .bad{border-left:3px solid var(--pink);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#f4d2c7;background:#2a1a18;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  svg{display:block;margin:0 auto;max-width:100%}
  svg text{fill:var(--ink);font-family:inherit}
  nav.toc{position:sticky;top:0;background:rgba(15,18,24,.93);backdrop-filter:blur(8px);
    border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);padding:.6rem 1rem;z-index:10;
    display:flex;gap:1rem;flex-wrap:wrap;font-size:.9rem}
  nav.toc a{color:var(--mute);text-decoration:none}
  nav.toc a:hover{color:var(--yellow)}
  /* sticky sidebar TOC (wide screens only; narrow screens fall back to top-nav) */
  aside.sidetoc{position:fixed;top:4.2rem;
    left:max(1rem, calc(50% - 440px - 220px));
    width:200px;max-height:calc(100vh - 6rem);overflow-y:auto;
    padding-right:1rem;border-right:1px solid var(--line);
    font-size:.88rem;z-index:5}
  aside.sidetoc .sidetoc-title{font-size:.7rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute);margin:0 0 .5rem;padding-bottom:.35rem;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line)}
  aside.sidetoc ol{list-style:none;padding:0;margin:0}
  aside.sidetoc li{margin:0}
  aside.sidetoc a{display:block;padding:.3rem .5rem;color:var(--mute);text-decoration:none;
    border-left:2px solid transparent;line-height:1.35;
    transition:color 120ms ease, border-color 120ms ease}
  aside.sidetoc a:hover{color:var(--ink)}
  aside.sidetoc a.active{color:var(--yellow);border-left-color:var(--yellow)}
  @media (max-width:1180px){ aside.sidetoc{display:none} }
  @media (min-width:1181px){ nav.toc a[href^="#"]{display:none} }
  .hero{padding:3rem 0 1rem}
  .small{font-size:.88rem;color:var(--mute)}
  footer{color:var(--mute);font-size:.88rem;text-align:center;margin-top:4rem}
  table.plain{width:100%;border-collapse:collapse;font-size:.95rem;margin:.6rem 0}
  table.plain th,table.plain td{padding:.35rem .5rem;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);text-align:left}
  table.plain th{color:var(--mute);font-weight:500}
  /* callback-css-auto-begin */
  aside.callback{
    margin:1.2rem 0;padding:.7rem 1rem;
    background:rgba(88,196,221,0.05);
    border-left:3px solid color-mix(in srgb, var(--cyan) 45%, transparent);
    border-radius:0 6px 6px 0;
    font-size:.93rem;
  }
  aside.callback .ttl{
    font-size:.68rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);margin-bottom:.3rem;font-weight:600;
  }
  aside.callback ul{margin:0;padding-left:1.1rem}
  aside.callback ul li{margin:.15rem 0}
  aside.callback a{color:inherit}
/* callback-css-auto-end */
  details.changelog{
    margin:2rem 0 1rem;padding:.6rem .9rem;
    background:rgba(0,0,0,0.22);
    border-radius:6px;
    font-size:.85rem;
  }
  details.changelog summary{
    cursor:pointer;letter-spacing:.1em;text-transform:uppercase;
    font-size:.7rem;color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);
  }
  details.changelog table{margin-top:.5rem;border-collapse:collapse;width:100%;font-size:.82rem}
  details.changelog td{padding:.2rem .6rem;border-bottom:1px dashed rgba(255,255,255,0.07);vertical-align:top}
  details.changelog td:first-child{color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);white-space:nowrap;font-variant-numeric:tabular-nums}
  aside.related{
    margin:1.2rem 0;padding:.7rem 1rem;
    background:color-mix(in srgb, var(--mute) 6%, transparent);
    border-left:3px solid color-mix(in srgb, var(--mute) 55%, transparent);
    border-radius:0 6px 6px 0;
    font-size:.93rem;
  }
  aside.related .ttl{
    font-size:.68rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);margin-bottom:.3rem;font-weight:600;
  }
  aside.related > div{margin:.15rem 0}
  aside.related .more{color:var(--mute);font-style:italic}
  aside.related a{color:inherit}
  /* display-prefs-css-auto-begin */
  html[data-hide-widgets] .widget { display: none !important; }
  html[data-hide-quizzes] .quiz { display: none !important; }
  html[data-hide-lineage] .lineage-strip { display: none !important; }
  .mv-display-toggles { display: inline-flex; gap: .6rem; align-items: center; }
  .mv-display-toggle { background: transparent; border: 1px solid var(--line, #333); border-radius: 6px; padding: 5px 10px; cursor: pointer; font-size: 1.05rem; line-height: 1; color: inherit; transition: background 120ms ease, border-color 120ms ease; }
  .mv-display-toggle:hover { background: var(--panel, rgba(255,255,255,0.06)); border-color: var(--mute, #666); }
  .mv-display-toggle--off { opacity: 0.35; }
  /* display-prefs-css-auto-end */

← Notebook
1 Sieves 2 Grothendieck topology axioms 3 Examples: small/large Zariski, étale, fpqc 4 Sheaves and sheafification 5 $\mathrm{Sh}(C, J)$ as a topos 6 Geometric morphisms from morphisms of sites

Grothendieck topologies sites

Coverings, sheafification, and Grothendieck toposes via sites $(C, J)$.

blocks.html:
1. Sieves

If you have already met presheaves on a topological space as functors $\mathrm{Open}(X)^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathsf{Set}$, the move to a Grothendieck site replaces the poset $\mathrm{Open}(X)$ by an arbitrary small category $C$ and replaces "open cover of an open set $U$" by something more flexible. The new building block is a sieve. A sieve $S$ on an object $c \in C$ is a set of arrows with codomain $c$ that is closed under precomposition: whenever $f\colon d \to c$ lies in $S$ and $g\colon e \to d$ is any arrow, the composite $f \circ g$ also lies in $S$. The closure condition is the categorical analogue of "if $V \subseteq U$ is in your cover and $W \subseteq V$, then $W$ is too" — except now $V \to U$ need not be a subset inclusion.

The slick reformulation, which makes everything click, is this: a sieve on $c$ is the same data as a subfunctor $S \hookrightarrow h_c$ of the representable presheaf $h_c = \operatorname{Hom}_C(-, c)$. Indeed, $h_c(d) = \{\text{arrows } d \to c\}$, and a subfunctor picks out, for each $d$, a subset $S(d) \subseteq h_c(d)$ stable under reindexing along $g\colon e \to d$ — which is exactly the precomposition-closure condition. So sieves on $c$ form a complete lattice $\mathrm{Sub}(h_c)$ inside the presheaf topos $\widehat{C} = [C^{\mathrm{op}}, \mathsf{Set}]$, with $\bigcup$ and $\bigcap$ computed pointwise.

Two extreme cases anchor the picture. The maximal sieve $\mathrm{max}_c$ contains every arrow into $c$, i.e. $\mathrm{max}_c = h_c$ itself. The empty sieve $\varnothing_c$ contains no arrows. A general sieve sits strictly between these, and any family $\{f_i\colon d_i \to c\}_{i \in I}$ generates a sieve — namely the smallest sieve containing each $f_i$, obtained by closing up under precomposition. This is the technical fact that lets us say "covering family" and "covering sieve" interchangeably: a Grothendieck topology can be specified either by listing covering families (a basis) or by listing the sieves they generate.

When $C$ is a poset (every hom-set has $\leq 1$ element), sieves degenerate beautifully: a sieve on $c$ is exactly a down-set $S \subseteq \{\,d : d \leq c\,\}$ of objects below $c$. The lattice $\mathrm{Sub}(h_c)$ becomes the lattice of down-sets, and the maximal sieve is the principal down-set $\downarrow\!c$. The widget below lets you toggle which down-sets of a small poset count as "covering" — illustrating that a Grothendieck topology is, in this case, just a choice of distinguished collection of down-sets.

See also
  • Categories and morphisms
  • Presheaves on $\mathrm{Open}(X)$

Sieves on a poset: down-sets and subfunctors of $h_c$
click an object to see its representable; click arrows to toggle a sieve
Pick an object $c$ to highlight $h_c$.
params.bodyScript:
<script>
/* =============================== SECTION 1 widget — sieves as subfunctors of h_c =============================== */
(function(){
  const svg = $('#svg-sieves-poset'), out = $('#out-sieves-poset');
  const mode = $('#sv-mode'), reset = $('#sv-reset');
  // 5-object poset: top object t, two mids a,b, two leaves x,y
  // Hasse: x < a < t, y < a < t, y < b < t  (so x ≤ a ≤ t; y ≤ a ≤ t; y ≤ b ≤ t)
  // Plus reflexive: every object ≤ itself.
  const pts = {
    t: {x:240, y:40, label:'t'},
    a: {x:160, y:110, label:'a'},
    b: {x:320, y:110, label:'b'},
    x: {x:120, y:180, label:'x'},
    y: {x:240, y:180, label:'y'}
  };
  // Hasse covers (immediate ≤)
  const covers = [['x','a'],['y','a'],['y','b'],['a','t'],['b','t']];
  // Transitive closure: pairs (d,c) with d ≤ c, d≠c
  function le(){
    const adj = new Map(Object.keys(pts).map(o=>[o,new Set([o])]));
    for(const [d,c] of covers) adj.get(d).add(c);
    let changed = true;
    while(changed){ changed=false;
      for(const u of adj.keys()) for(const v of adj.get(u))
        for(const w of adj.get(v)) if(!adj.get(u).has(w)){ adj.get(u).add(w); changed=true; }
    }
    return adj;
  }
  const order = le();
  // All arrows d -> c with d ≤ c
  const arrows = [];
  for(const c of Object.keys(pts)) for(const d of order.keys())
    if(order.get(d).has(c)) arrows.push({d,c, key:`${d}->${c}`});
  let selected = 't';     // representable mode
  let sieve = new Set();  // build mode: keys of in-sieve arrows
  function isCovering(){
    // A sieve is "covering" if it contains the maximal sieve, i.e. all arrows into selected
    const inc = arrows.filter(a => a.c === selected).map(a => a.key);
    return inc.every(k => sieve.has(k));
  }
  function downClosed(){
    // After toggling, force precomposition closure: if (d->c) in sieve and (e->d) exists, add (e->c)
    let again = true;
    while(again){ again=false;
      for(const a of arrows) if(sieve.has(a.key)){
        for(const a2 of arrows) if(a2.c === a.d && !sieve.has(`${a2.d}->${a.c}`)){
          sieve.add(`${a2.d}->${a.c}`); again=true;
        }
      }
    }
  }
  function draw(){
    svg.innerHTML = "<title>Sieves on a poset Hasse diagram</title>";
    // Title text
    const ttl = SVG('text',{x:240,y:18,'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':12,fill:'var(--mute)'});
    ttl.textContent = mode.value === 'repr'
      ? `representable on c = ${selected}: arrows into ${selected} (in blue)`
      : `building a sieve on ${selected} — click arrows to toggle`;
    svg.appendChild(ttl);
    // Edges (Hasse covers — drawn unobtrusively)
    for(const [d,c] of covers){
      const p1 = pts[d], p2 = pts[c];
      const ln = SVG('line',{x1:p1.x,y1:p1.y,x2:p2.x,y2:p2.y,stroke:'var(--line)','stroke-width':1});
      svg.appendChild(ln);
    }
    // All arrows d -> c (transitively): drawn dim, then highlighted by mode
    for(const a of arrows){
      if(a.d === a.c) continue; // skip identity for visual clarity
      const inHc = (a.c === selected);
      const inSieve = sieve.has(a.key);
      let color = 'var(--mute)', width = 0.8, dash = '2,3';
      if(mode.value === 'repr' && inHc){ color = 'var(--blue)'; width = 1.6; dash = null; }
      if(mode.value === 'build' && a.c === selected){
        if(inSieve){ color = 'var(--yellow)'; width = 1.8; dash = null; }
        else { color = 'var(--mute)'; width = 1.0; dash = '2,3'; }
      }
      const p1 = pts[a.d], p2 = pts[a.c];
      // curve slightly so concentric arrows don't overlap
      const dx = p2.x - p1.x, dy = p2.y - p1.y;
      const L = Math.hypot(dx,dy) || 1;
      const nx = -dy/L, ny = dx/L;
      const k = (a.d.charCodeAt(0) + a.c.charCodeAt(0)) % 5 - 2;
      const mx = (p1.x+p2.x)/2 + nx*k*5;
      const my = (p1.y+p2.y)/2 + ny*k*5;
      const path = SVG('path',{
        d:`M${p1.x} ${p1.y} Q ${mx} ${my} ${p2.x} ${p2.y}`,
        fill:'none', stroke:color, 'stroke-width':width
      });
      if(dash) path.setAttribute('stroke-dasharray', dash);
      path.style.cursor = (mode.value === 'build' && a.c === selected) ? 'pointer' : 'default';
      if(mode.value === 'build' && a.c === selected){
        path.addEventListener('click', ()=>{
          if(sieve.has(a.key)) sieve.delete(a.key);
          else sieve.add(a.key);
          downClosed();
          draw();
        });
      }
      svg.appendChild(path);
    }
    // Nodes
    for(const o of Object.keys(pts)){
      const p = pts[o];
      const isSel = (o === selected);
      const c = SVG('circle',{cx:p.x,cy:p.y,r:14,
        fill: isSel ? 'rgba(255,216,102,0.18)' : 'var(--panel2)',
        stroke: isSel ? 'var(--yellow)' : 'var(--blue)',
        'stroke-width':1.6});
      c.style.cursor = 'pointer';
      c.addEventListener('click', ()=>{ selected = o; sieve = new Set(); draw(); });
      svg.appendChild(c);
      const t = SVG('text',{x:p.x,y:p.y+5,'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':12,
        fill: isSel ? 'var(--yellow)' : 'var(--ink)','font-weight':600});
      t.textContent = p.label;
      svg.appendChild(t);
    }
    // Readout
    if(mode.value === 'repr'){
      const incoming = arrows.filter(a=>a.c===selected);
      out.textContent = `h_${selected}(d) = arrows d → ${selected}.   |incoming| = ${incoming.length}: ${incoming.map(a=>a.key).join(', ')}`;
    } else {
      const inc = arrows.filter(a=>a.c===selected);
      const have = inc.filter(a=>sieve.has(a.key)).map(a=>a.key);
      out.textContent = `sieve on ${selected}: { ${have.join(', ') || '∅'} }   ` +
        `${have.length === inc.length && inc.length > 0 ? '— maximal (= h_'+selected+')' : ''}`;
    }
  }
  mode.addEventListener('change', ()=>{ sieve = new Set(); draw(); });
  reset.addEventListener('click', ()=>{ sieve = new Set(); selected = 't'; mode.value='repr'; draw(); });
  draw();
})();
</script>

blocks.html:
2. Grothendieck topology axioms

A Grothendieck topology $J$ on a small category $C$ assigns to every object $c \in C$ a collection $J(c)$ of sieves on $c$, called the covering sieves. The pair $(C, J)$ is called a site. The data $J$ must satisfy three axioms. (M) Maximality: the maximal sieve $\mathrm{max}_c = h_c$ is in $J(c)$ — every $c$ is covered by "all arrows into $c$." (S) Stability under pullback: if $S \in J(c)$ and $f\colon d \to c$ is any arrow, then the pullback sieve $f^*S = \{\,g\colon e \to d : f \circ g \in S\,\}$ lies in $J(d)$. (T) Transitivity / local character: if $S \in J(c)$ and $T$ is any sieve on $c$ such that $f^*T \in J(d)$ for every $f\colon d \to c$ in $S$, then $T \in J(c)$.

Each axiom encodes a familiar property of open covers. Maximality says "every space covers itself." Stability is the restriction property: pulling a cover back along $f$ gives a cover of the source. Transitivity is the refinement property — if a presumed cover $T$ becomes a genuine cover after restricting to each piece of an actual cover $S$, then $T$ was already a cover. Together these are the minimal data needed for the sheaf condition (limits over covers) to behave well: stability gives functoriality of restriction, transitivity gives local-to-global gluing, maximality is the trivial "self-cover."

The cleanest sanity check is the chaotic / trivial topology $J_{\mathrm{triv}}$, which declares $J(c) = \{\mathrm{max}_c\}$ for every $c$ — only the maximal sieve covers. All three axioms hold: M is given; for S, $f^*\mathrm{max}_c = \mathrm{max}_d$ since every arrow into $d$ composes into $\mathrm{max}_c$; for T, the only candidate $T$ that pulls back to maximal sieves on every leg of $\mathrm{max}_c$ is $\mathrm{max}_c$ itself. So $J_{\mathrm{triv}}$ is a Grothendieck topology — sheaves on $(C, J_{\mathrm{triv}})$ are exactly all presheaves, since there is nothing nontrivial to glue. At the other extreme, the discrete topology $J_{\mathrm{disc}}$ declares every sieve covering — and only the constant presheaf at the terminal set is a sheaf.

The walk-through below verifies all three axioms on a small worked example: the 3-element poset $\{x, y, t\}$ with $x \leq t$ and $y \leq t$, equipped with the topology where $\{x \to t, y \to t\}$ generates a covering sieve on $t$. Scrub through to see how M, S, and T each constrain (or don't) on this site.

3. Examples: small/large Zariski, étale, fpqc

The motivating examples come from algebraic geometry. Fix a scheme $X$. The small Zariski site $X_{\mathrm{Zar}}$ has, as objects, open subschemes $U \hookrightarrow X$, with arrows the open immersions; covers are the usual Zariski open covers. This recovers ordinary sheaf theory on the underlying topological space — sheaves on $X_{\mathrm{Zar}}$ are sheaves on $|X|$. The small étale site $X_{\mathrm{ét}}$ relaxes the morphisms: objects are étale $X$-schemes $U \to X$ (formally smooth + locally of finite presentation + unramified), and a family $\{U_i \to U\}$ covers if it is jointly surjective. Étale maps include open immersions but also finite separable extensions of fields and unramified covers, so $X_{\mathrm{ét}}$ sees Galois-style data invisible to $X_{\mathrm{Zar}}$.

The fpqc site goes further still: covers are jointly surjective families $\{U_i \to U\}$ where each $U_i \to U$ is faithfully flat and quasi-compact (no smoothness or finite-type hypothesis at all). Even fpqc covers are subsumed by the much larger fppf site (faithfully flat + locally of finite presentation), but for descent of quasi-coherent sheaves the canonical choice is fpqc — this is the content of fpqc descent, the cornerstone of modern algebraic geometry. The "small" versus "large" distinction is whether the site lives over a single $X$ or ranges over all schemes; the large étale site of $X$ has all $X$-schemes as objects, with étale covers.

The point of having multiple sites is that local triviality means different things in each. A line bundle on $X$ is, by definition, a quasi-coherent sheaf that is Zariski-locally free of rank one — every line bundle is Zariski-locally trivial. But a $\mu_n$-torsor — a sheaf classifying $n$th roots of unity — need not be Zariski-locally trivial: on $\operatorname{Spec} \mathbb{F}_2$, the field has only one square root of $1$ (namely $1$), so the trivialization $\mu_2 \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathbb{Z}/2$ is not Zariski-local. You have to pass to the étale site (extracting a separable closure) before $\mu_2$ becomes constant. This is the Galois descent phenomenon: the difference between Zariski-locally trivial and étale-locally trivial is precisely the failure of a sheaf to descend along finite separable extensions.

The diagram below toggles between the Zariski, étale, and fpqc covers of $\operatorname{Spec} R$ for $R = \mathbb{Z}[1/n]$ — watch how the cover sets coarsen as you climb the tower, and how a $\mu_3$-torsor that's invisible Zariski (no third roots of unity in $\mathbb{Q}$) snaps into focus once you pass to the étale site.

4. Sheaves and sheafification

A presheaf $F\colon C^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathsf{Set}$ is a sheaf on the site $(C, J)$ if for every covering sieve $S \in J(c)$, the restriction $F(c) \to \prod_{f \in S} F(\mathrm{dom}(f))$ exhibits $F(c)$ as the limit of $F$ over the sieve $S$. Equivalently: every "matching family" $\{x_f \in F(\mathrm{dom}(f))\}_{f \in S}$ — a family compatible under further restriction $g\colon e \to \mathrm{dom}(f)$, meaning $F(g)(x_f) = x_{f \circ g}$ — has a unique amalgamation $x \in F(c)$ with $F(f)(x) = x_f$ for every $f \in S$. The "exists" half is gluing; the "unique" half is separation. When $C = \mathrm{Open}(X)$ this is precisely the sheaf axiom you've already met.

The sheafification functor $a\colon \widehat{C} \to \mathrm{Sh}(C, J)$ is the left adjoint to the inclusion $\iota\colon \mathrm{Sh}(C, J) \hookrightarrow \widehat{C}$. Concretely, it's built by Grothendieck's plus-construction, applied twice: $a(F) = (F^+)^+$, where $F^+(c) = \mathrm{colim}_{S \in J(c)} \mathrm{Match}(S, F)$ is the colimit over covering sieves of matching families on $S$. The first $(-)^+$ produces a separated presheaf (gluings unique when they exist); the second $(-)^+$ adds the missing gluings. Iterating once more is idempotent, so two passes suffice — a striking elementary fact whose closest analogue is the sheafification of a presheaf on a topological space via stalks.

Not every presheaf is a sheaf, even on familiar sites. The classic non-example is the constant presheaf $\underline{A}_{\mathrm{pre}}$ on $X_{\mathrm{Zar}}$ for $X$ disconnected: $\underline{A}_{\mathrm{pre}}(U) = A$ regardless of $U$. If $X = U_1 \sqcup U_2$ with both pieces non-empty, the matching family $(a_1, a_2) \in A \times A$ over the disjoint cover has no amalgamation in $\underline{A}_{\mathrm{pre}}(X) = A$ unless $a_1 = a_2$, so gluing fails. Sheafifying gives the locally constant sheaf $\underline{A}$, with $\underline{A}(U) = A^{\pi_0(U)}$ — one copy of $A$ per connected component.

The case library below collects four presheaves on three different sites and tells you, for each (site, presheaf) pair, whether it's a sheaf — and if not, what the obstruction is. Notice the site-dependence: a presheaf can be a sheaf on Zariski but fail on étale, or vice versa. This is the same site-dependence that drove §3.

  • Presheaves on Open(X)
  • Sheafification on a topological space

  const svg = $('#svg-sheaf-cases'), out = $('#out-sheaf-cases');
  const ph = $('#sh-presheaf'), st = $('#sh-site');
  // verdict[presheaf][site] = { isSheaf, why }
  const V = {
    rep: {
      zar:  { isSheaf:true,  why:'Yoneda gives $\\operatorname{Hom}(-, T)$ a sheaf on $X_{\\mathrm{Zar}}$ — locally given morphisms agree on overlaps and glue.' },
      et:   { isSheaf:true,  why:'Representables are sheaves on the étale site (this is descent for morphisms — Grothendieck, FGA).' },
      fpqc: { isSheaf:true,  why:'Representables are sheaves on the fpqc site (fpqc descent for morphisms).' }
    },
    constpre: {
      zar:  { isSheaf:false, why:'Fails on disconnected $U$: matching family $(a_1, a_2)$ over $U = U_1 \\sqcup U_2$ has no amalgamation unless $a_1 = a_2$.' },
      et:   { isSheaf:false, why:'Same failure, even worse: étale covers can disconnect things further.' },
      fpqc: { isSheaf:false, why:'Same failure on disconnected covers; sheafification gives the locally constant sheaf.' }
    },
    locconst: {
      zar:  { isSheaf:true,  why:'$\\underline{A}(U) = A^{\\pi_0(U)}$ glues correctly: connected components match across covers.' },
      et:   { isSheaf:true,  why:'Étale-locally connected components are stable, so $\\underline{A}$ is a sheaf.' },
      fpqc: { isSheaf:true,  why:'Locally constant sheaves descend along faithfully flat covers.' }
    },
    mu3: {
      zar:  { isSheaf:true,  why:'$\\mu_3 = \\operatorname{Spec} \\mathbb{Z}[T]/(T^3-1)$ is representable, hence a Zariski sheaf — but it is NOT Zariski-locally trivial (no $\\zeta_3 \\in \\mathbb{Q}$).' },
      et:   { isSheaf:true,  why:'Still a sheaf — and now ALSO étale-locally trivial (after adjoining $\\zeta_3$). This is Galois descent.' },
      fpqc: { isSheaf:true,  why:'Sheaf and trivial after a faithfully flat cover; same as étale here.' }
    }
  };
  function draw(){
    svg.innerHTML = "<title>Sheaf check verdict</title>";
    const v = V[ph.value][st.value];
    const fill = v.isSheaf ? 'rgba(131,193,103,0.18)' : 'rgba(224,122,95,0.18)';
    const stroke = v.isSheaf ? 'var(--green)' : 'var(--pink)';
    const txt = v.isSheaf ? '✓ a sheaf' : '✗ NOT a sheaf';
    svg.appendChild(SVG('rect',{x:120,y:25,width:240,height:50,rx:8,ry:8,fill,stroke,'stroke-width':1.8}));
    const t = SVG('text',{x:240,y:55,'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':16,fill:stroke,'font-weight':700});
    t.textContent = txt;
    svg.appendChild(t);
    out.innerHTML = v.why;
    if(typeof renderMathInElement === 'function'){
      renderMathInElement(out, { delimiters:[{left:'$',right:'$',display:false}], throwOnError:false });
    }
  }
  ph.addEventListener('change', draw);
  st.addEventListener('change', draw);
  draw();
})();
</script>

blocks.html:
5. $\mathrm{Sh}(C, J)$ as a topos

The category $\mathrm{Sh}(C, J)$ of sheaves on a site is a Grothendieck topos. It is automatically an elementary topos — it has all finite limits, it is cartesian closed, and it has a subobject classifier $\Omega$ — and on top of those finitary properties it has all small colimits and a small generating set (the representables, sheafified). Giraud's theorem is the converse: a category $\mathcal{E}$ is equivalent to $\mathrm{Sh}(C, J)$ for some site $(C, J)$ iff (i) $\mathcal{E}$ has all small colimits, (ii) finite limits commute with filtered colimits in $\mathcal{E}$, (iii) $\mathcal{E}$ has a small generating set, (iv) all colimits are universal (stable under pullback), (v) sums in $\mathcal{E}$ are disjoint, and (vi) every equivalence relation is effective. These six axioms, the Giraud axioms, intrinsically characterize Grothendieck toposes among elementary toposes.

Examples illustrate how broadly this captures geometry. For a topological space $X$, $\mathrm{Sh}(X) := \mathrm{Sh}(\mathrm{Open}(X), \mathrm{open\ covers})$ is the topos of sheaves on $X$ — when $X$ is sober, $\mathrm{Sh}(X)$ is essentially the same as $X$. Taking $C$ to be a one-object category, sheaves are just sets and we recover $\mathsf{Set}$ as the terminal Grothendieck topos. Taking $C = G$ a group (one object, arrows are group elements) gives the topos $G\text{-}\mathsf{Set}$ of $G$-sets. The topos $\mathrm{Sh}(\operatorname{Spec} k)$ for a field $k$ is $\mathsf{Set}$ when $k$ is separably closed, and $\operatorname{Gal}(k^{\mathrm{sep}}/k)\text{-}\mathsf{Set}_{\mathrm{cts}}$ in general — Galois theory becomes topos theory.

The presheaf topos $\widehat{C}$ is the special case of the trivial topology on $C$. Inside $\widehat{C}$ the sheaves form a reflective subcategory via the sheafification adjunction $a \dashv \iota$, and $a$ preserves finite limits — making $\iota$ a geometric embedding. Conversely, every geometric embedding $\mathcal{E} \hookrightarrow \widehat{C}$ is sheafification for a uniquely determined topology $J$ on $C$ (Lawvere–Tierney's theorem on subtoposes — equivalently, on Lawvere–Tierney topologies $j\colon \Omega \to \Omega$). So Grothendieck topologies, geometric embeddings into a presheaf topos, and Lawvere–Tierney topologies are three faces of the same coin.

The illustration below summarises the Giraud axioms as a closed loop: each axiom corresponds to a structural property a Grothendieck topos has, and together they reconstruct the site.

  • What is a topos?
  • Geometric morphisms
  • Heyting algebras and the internal logic

blocks.html:
6. Geometric morphisms from morphisms of sites

A geometric morphism $f\colon \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{E}$ between toposes is an adjunction $f^* \dashv f_*$ where the inverse image $f^*\colon \mathcal{E} \to \mathcal{F}$ preserves finite limits. The direct image $f_*\colon \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{E}$ then automatically preserves all limits (it has a left adjoint). The asymmetry — that $f^*$ is required to be left exact, while $f_*$ has no symmetric finiteness requirement — is what makes "geometric morphism" the right notion: for a continuous map $\varphi\colon X \to Y$ of topological spaces, the induced $\mathrm{Sh}(\varphi)\colon \mathrm{Sh}(X) \to \mathrm{Sh}(Y)$ is a geometric morphism with $f^* = \varphi^{-1}$ (pullback of sheaves) and $f_* = \varphi_*$ (direct image sheaf). The opposite asymmetry would invert which-way maps go.

Site theory provides the canonical mechanism for building geometric morphisms. A continuous map of sites $u\colon (D, K) \to (C, J)$ is a functor $u\colon C \to D$ (note the direction!) that takes covering sieves to covering sieves: if $S \in J(c)$ then $u(S)$ generates a covering sieve in $K(u(c))$. Continuity is exactly the condition that the obvious "restrict along $u$" presheaf functor $u^p\colon \widehat{D} \to \widehat{C}$ preserves the sheaf condition. After sheafifying, this gives a geometric morphism $\mathrm{Sh}(D, K) \to \mathrm{Sh}(C, J)$ where the inverse image is (left Kan extension along $u$, then sheafify) and the direct image is (precompose with $u$).

The construction is functorial: a composition $C \xrightarrow{u} D \xrightarrow{v} E$ of continuous functors of sites induces a composition of geometric morphisms in the opposite direction $\mathrm{Sh}(E) \to \mathrm{Sh}(D) \to \mathrm{Sh}(C)$. This is exactly the mechanism by which a continuous map $X \to Y$ of schemes gives a geometric morphism $X_{\mathrm{ét}} \to Y_{\mathrm{ét}}$ at the level of étale toposes — and in turn how the étale fundamental group $\pi_1^{\mathrm{ét}}(X)$ is recovered from the slice topos. Not every geometric morphism between sheaf toposes comes from a morphism of sites in this strict sense, but enough do — and this is the workhorse construction of Grothendieck-style algebraic geometry.

The widget below builds a geometric morphism from a continuous functor of sites and lets you trace what $f^*$ and $f_*$ do to a specific sheaf on the target.

See also
  • Geometric morphisms (intro)
  • Étale cohomology
